So, let's go through this in more detail now. Let's get specific here with the structure. Okay, now there are some basic core steps here we're going to go through. First, like everything else with the straight line, you have to do your homework in advance. In other words, know what your qualifying questions are and what your outcome for each question specifically is. Write down the perfect set of questions and memorize them. Now, if you're one of those lucky people who's simply selling strictly by the phone, you can actually have your questions written down in sequence right in front of you. But if you can't, then listen, just do the work and you actually got to memorize your questions. And as you do this, make sure that you set your questions up in a logical chain. See, this is what a lot of salespeople miss. They tend to go all over the place and really don't follow this little logical question asking. So, one of the first core rules here, number one, which starts from going off into Pluto or your Aedis, number one, you start off with big picture questions. That's the first thing you want to ask. You don't go to the specific first, you start off with your big picture questions. So, let me give you an example here of some really powerful big picture questions. And of course, you have to figure out which ones are exactly suited to your particular industry here. So, the first big picture question would be number one, what do you like or dislike with the current product you're using? Now, that would work really well, for instance, if the person that you're prospecting has a product that's similar to yours or is used to something similar in the past, right? So, if I ask them what do they like or dislike with it, they'll simply tell you all the features or the benefits that are important to them. They'll basically say, listen, I didn't like it for A, B, C or D reasons. So, of course, you know from that that you better be very careful that when you're presenting your product, you don't frame it that it's just like the one they did not like, right? That's a very, very good example of a big picture question. Essentially, what they'll do is they'll actually customize their product for you. They'll give you the keys to the kingdom of exactly what it is that they want, okay? Now, another big picture question would be, you know, what would you change or improve as your current source? Now, imagine that one. Let's say they have the supplier already. This tells you where their pain is, what's wrong with the product they're currently using, okay? And it's very, very powerful here to see, you know, where your client is frustrated and the things that are kind of getting under his fingernails right now in the moment. Third big picture question, what's your biggest headache with your current insurance company, your current IT provider, your current stockbroker, your current whatever it is? See, it doesn't matter what the product is here, but this is one of those big picture questions that identifies pain. And if they say to you, well, you know, it's not really that big a deal. You know, they try to minimize the pain. You can probe a bit deeper and you say, well, let me ask you a question. You know, how has this impacted you in the past and how to see this going for you in the future? Do you see getting better or worse? You know, that's how you really start to amplify a person's pain. And again, people love to talk about their pain. They love to talk about their tale of woe. Give them a second. Let them think about it. And believe me, they will start to tell you all about the things that are wrong in their life. Now, don't say to them, wow, so I see a lot of pain in your life there. Come on, tell me about your pain. I really want to know what's your pain? What's your pain? Come on, give me some pain. If you do that, you basically broken any rapport. You destroyed any rapport that you've built up to this point. Right. So remember you'll use your tonality. So it's, you know, so what's your biggest headache with, you know, tell me, I really want to know, you know, it's not like, you know, what's your biggest thing? Ha, ha. So what's your biggest headache? You know, in the unspoken words are, you know, I'm really interested. I really do care. I'd really like to know. Again, with these questions here, it's always the tonality that carries the day that either positions you as the grand inquisitor who they want, knock over the head or the trusted advisor family friend who is there to help them resolve them, their pain and put them on the road to pleasure. Okay. Another big picture question. Number four, what's your ultimate objective? Now, meaning what do they really do to accomplish here? And again, this is one that screwed me up that when I talked about my experience where I didn't ask them what their goals were. In other words, I use my own map of the world to figure out what I thought they needed. Okay. Versus to figure out what they really did it need by asking question. Remember, we think that what's important to us is what's important to everybody else. And the truth is, is that's really not the way the world works. You know, what's important to you is not important to the next guy, right? Next, here's another great one. What would your ideal program be? If you could design this for yourself, you know, what would it be? And again, now, they'll start giving you all the nuts and bolts of what they want. This is really saying, hey, show me how to tailor my product to directly suit your needs. Remember, people will always pay more for something that's customized for them. So finding out what's important to them, what all their little needs are, step by step, bam, that's really, really crucial. Okay. Another big picture question here of all the factors you mentioned so far. What's the most important one to you? Now, what we're doing is we're establishing their hierarchy of needs. So they might have three or four or five different needs, but there's one overwhelming need that really trumps everything else. So again, this is a question that helps us identify their hierarchy of needs. And when you do this, it really positions you when you're running your loops later on. We're going to, again, go through that module later when you're looping to understand really truly what is important. Most second to most, third to most, bam, it's really the keys to the kingdom of influence. And this leads me to another great one, which is, have I asked about every detail that's important to you? That's where I left anything out. Sometimes when you say this, they'll be like, why? They've never even heard it before. They're like, wow, like this is not your typical salesperson. What does that really say? That means that I care. Like, I really want to know if I left anything out, anything else you'd like to tell them before I, you know, want to solve your problem. It's the expert's approach. Is there anything else I've left out, anything you want to tell me? And when you do that right with the right tonality, you get such a tight rapport with somebody that by the time you're done with these big picture questions, you literally own them in terms of, you know, in terms of the cruising control here and how your position is an expert sharp as a tap, all the things that really, really matter here. Okay. And again, you know, what you don't want to be is one of these other salesmen who's just, you know, a cliched, how much money you have in the bank, how many business for what proxy you need, blah, blah, blah. And before you know it, the person's already tuned out and your ability to influence has really been minimized to the point of absurdity.
